turns.content: I'm building a library and I want to know what the actual difference between setting MSRV in Cargo.toml and using the cargo-msrv tool for testing.

The MSRV field in your Cargo.toml is just a metadata declaration that tells downstream developers or automated tools like CI systems what the lowest supported Rust edition version is, but it doesn't actually enforce compatibility at build time. The cargo-msrv tool actively runs your crate against older toolchains to detect if compilation fails, which means you can catch regressions before they hit users even if you haven't updated your MSRV yet.
So I should just ignore the Cargo.toml field and only rely on running cargo-msrv in CI?

Not quite, there is a nuance here. The MSRV setting in your Cargo.toml acts as documentation for consumers of your crate so they know which toolchain versions are supported without needing to run extra checks themselves, whereas cargo-msrv is an active safety mechanism that prevents you from accidentally lowering the compatibility threshold unexpectedly.

I think I misunderstood earlier. If I set MSRV to 1.75 in Cargo.toml but my code actually compiles on 1.60, then cargo-msrv will still fail when it checks the old toolchain and forces me to update the MSRV field.

That is exactly right. The cargo-msrv tool will detect that your crate fails on the older version 1.60, which means you must either fix the code or raise the minimum supported Rust edition version in Cargo.toml to reflect reality.
I'm looking at this crate's Cargo.toml and I see the field for minimum supported version, but how exactly does it interact with my CI pipeline?

That field tells cargo which toolchain to assume when building, so your tests will only run against that specific compiler. In a CI system you need to explicitly test against multiple versions of the rustc binary to ensure compatibility.
I don't get it yet. I'm used to setting up an environment and running a single build, so why would testing against different toolchains be necessary?

Because the Rust compiler evolves and new features may break older syntax or remove deprecated items in previous versions. If you only test on one version, your crate might silently fail when users try to compile with an older toolchain later.
So I should just add a dependency for cargo-msrv and run it manually? That seems like extra work without clear benefits.

Cargo-msrv automates that testing process by installing the correct toolchain versions required by your crate and running tests against each. It ensures your code compiles successfully on all supported versions, preventing regressions before release.
Wait, but what if my project has some legacy traits or types that aren't compatible with newer standards? Won't cargo-msrv just fail silently?

No, it will surface the exact version where your code stops compiling. You'll see which toolchain fails and can adjust your use of traits or types accordingly. It's better to know upfront what breaks than to have users report issues after deployment.

Imagine I'm writing a library that needs to support both old and new toolchains. I want to ensure compatibility without forcing users to upgrade their compilers immediately, so I'm thinking about setting the MSRV in Cargo.toml and using cargo-msrv to test my project against multiple versions.

That is a solid approach for managing backward compatibility. You should definitely define the minimum supported Rust version field in your Cargo.toml manifest, which acts as the primary signal for downstream users. When you run cargo-msrv locally to verify your code, it will compare the output of compiling against different toolchains to ensure no features are broken.

I see. So if I set MSRV to 1.70 in Cargo.toml and my CI runs cargo-msrv, it will automatically update the version field whenever a new stable release is published, right? That way I can keep my documentation accurate without manually updating every time.

You have that mostly correct regarding the automatic updates for stable releases. However, there is one critical detail you might be missing: cargo-msrv primarily focuses on testing against specific older versions to ensure your code still compiles and passes tests. It does not automatically rewrite your Cargo.toml metadata based on the latest stable release unless configured with a specific flag.
Ah, so I need to manually update that field in Cargo.toml whenever I want to declare support for a newer baseline? And then rely on cargo-msrv just to verify the code works against older versions?

Exactly. The workflow is typically: you set the MSRV field in Cargo.toml to reflect your current minimum requirement, and you use cargo-msrv with flags like --older or specific version lists to test compatibility against legacy toolchains before releasing.

I keep thinking MSRV is just like the minimum Python version you pin in requirements.txt, but my CI keeps failing on older toolchains because I haven't actually configured it properly. I'm trying to use cargo-msrv to test compatibility with multiple versions, but I don't understand how specifying a specific MSRV in Cargo.toml actually changes what gets tested or why the build errors are so verbose.

Don't worry, that's exactly where people get stuck when they treat MSRV like a simple version pin. The thing about cargo-msrv is it doesn't just read your Cargo.toml; it actively builds against different toolchains to verify compatibility, which means the output can be overwhelming if you aren't expecting it. When you specify MSRV in Cargo.toml, you're telling the compiler what's the lowest supported version, but the real magic happens when you run cargo-msrv with flags like --min or --max to define your exact range of toolchains to test against. This helps catch edge cases where a crate might break on specific older editions because it forces the build process to validate behavior across those versions systematically rather than just assuming everything works.
